L1.area: Native GIF image decoding and frame rasterization logic

L2.description: A double free vulnerability exists in the GIF decoding routine where, when a frame has a zero-size raster dimension, a reallocation call with size zero may implicitly free the underlying buffer and return NULL. The code then misinterprets this as an allocation failure without updating the buffer pointer, leading to the same memory being freed again on subsequent frames or cleanup. This can be exploited via a specially crafted GIF image to achieve arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

L3.area: Native C GIF frame decoding and raster buffer management during multi-frame GIF processing
L3.description: A double free vulnerability exists in the GIF frame decoding routine where, when processing a multi-frame GIF image with frames that have zero-width or zero-height dimensions, a reallocation of the raster pixel buffer with size zero may implicitly free the buffer and return NULL. The code misinterprets this NULL return as an out-of-memory condition without updating the stored buffer pointer, causing the already-freed buffer to be freed again during subsequent frame processing or resource cleanup. A remote attacker can exploit this by sending a specially crafted GIF image with at least three frames where later frames have zero-size dimensions.

ground_truth.reason: The DDGifSlurp function in decoding.c calls reallocarray with a zero size when a GIF frame has zero-dimension raster, which implicitly frees the rasterBits buffer and returns NULL. The code treats this as an allocation failure without nullifying the pointer, leading to a double free on subsequent operations.
